mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive coverage of sculpture as three-dimensional art: design elements and composition (A.1), iconography including religious and political expression (A.2), materials spanning stone, metal, wood, clay, glass, and found objects (A.3), techniques including carving, modelling, and casting (A.4), kinds of sculpture in the round, relief, and kinetic (B.1), representational and non-representational forms (B.2, B.2.a), and Western sculptural history from Greek classicism through Modernism (C.1).

The Pieta and David exemplify representational devotional and monumental human figure sculpture (B.2.a), executed in stone (A.3), with Renaissance religious iconography (A.2), within the Western sculptural tradition (C.1).
Bronze as a primary material of sculpture (A.3) widely used in casting (A.4), with historical significance from the Bronze Age through contemporary sculptural practice.
Clay as a material of sculpture (A.3), with decorated ceramic wares shading into decorative sculpture (B.2.c) across regional traditions.
Gold as a precious metal used in sculpture (A.3) and historically significant in coins and medals (B.3.b).
Prehistoric petroglyphs and rock art represent the earliest primitive and folk sculptural expressions (B.4) from Stone Age tool-making cultures.
Sculpture as one of the core static visual arts forms, situated within the broader context of fine arts versus applied/decorative arts distinctions relevant to the art of sculpture (A).

Encompasses sculpture within the broader history of aesthetic visual production across cultures and periods, directly relevant to the history of sculpture (C) and Western sculpture specifically (C.1).
Realism as truthful, non-idealized representation relates to representational sculpture (B.2.a) and its approach to human figures and subject matter.
The broader concept of art and its contested definitions, including the distinction between fine arts and applied arts, provides context for sculpture as an art form (A).
Iron as a metal material of sculpture (A.3), used in sculptural construction and welded assemblage.
Wood as one of the traditional materials of sculpture (A.3), used in carving across many cultural traditions.
Jewellery as decorative worn items relates to ceremonial and ritualistic sculptural objects (B.3.a) and overlaps with coins and medals as small-scale sculptural forms (B.3.b).
Active across sculpture as a High Renaissance polymath, contributing to the Western sculptural tradition (C.1) though primarily known for painting.
Color as a visual element relevant to design and composition in sculpture (A.1), particularly in polychrome and surface-finished works.

Performing arts traditions across African, Asian, and Western cultures use ceremonial masks and ritualistic objects that constitute a major category of sculptural production (B.3.a, B.3.d), connecting performing arts to sculptural function.
The extraction of stone, metals, and gemstones provides the raw materials of sculpture (A.3), though mining itself is an industrial rather than artistic activity.
